topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like tiny, sharp needles pricking my skin. I stood before the canvas, a swirling vortex of charcoal and indigo that was unequivocally mine, now hung in some pretentious, sterile space. My jaw tightened until a dull ache radiated through my temples. I watched the crowd mill about, their hushed murmurs a constant, irritating soundtrack to my mounting fury. Every glance directed at the piece felt like an accusation. I remembered the meticulous layering of pigment, the sleepless nights poured into those strokes, and now it was being casually admired, owned by strangers. A slow, cold resolve settled in my stomach, hardening into something sharp and unyielding. I didn't move immediately. Instead, I let my gaze sweep across the room, cataloging every face, every security guard, every potential avenue for disruption. When I finally turned, my expression was carefully blank, a mask of polite indifference that felt like polished ice.

[story 2]

"It's quite striking, isn't it?" a voice drifted over, dripping with false admiration. I felt a hot flush rise from my chest, quickly suppressed beneath a cool, deliberate calm. They didn't see the struggle it took to bring that vision into being. They saw only decoration, an object to be appreciated on their terms. My hands clenched into fists inside my coat pockets; the fabric strained against the sudden tension. I took a slow, measured breath, inhaling the scent of expensive perfume and misplaced entitlement. I walked toward the painting, my steps deliberate, each one a measured advance toward confrontation. When I reached the edge of the display, I didn't touch it, but I leaned in close, my shadow falling across the surface. My voice, when I spoke, was low, dangerously steady. "This is not yours to display." The silence that followed was heavy, charged, waiting for the inevitable fallout.

[story 3]

I watched them approach the installation like vultures circling carrion. The way they tilted their heads, the soft appreciation in their eyes—it was an insult wrapped in velvet. A tremor ran through my hands, a barely perceptible vibration I had to fight to keep from becoming a full-blown shake. I wanted to shatter the glass separating me from the art, to rip the frame from the wall and cast it into the gutter. Instead, I focused on the floor, mapping out the exits, the structural weaknesses of the room. My breathing became shallow, controlled, a silent engine powering a deep, internal churning. When the curator finally approached, smiling that practiced, patronizing smile, I met his gaze. My eyes held a depth of cold resentment that I hoped he couldn't decipher. "I require that piece back," I stated, the words flat, devoid of any warmth, letting the sheer force of my demand hang in the air like a suffocating shroud.
